blocks.text: The word of the Lord that came to Micah of Moresheth in the days of Kings Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah of Judah, which he saw concerning Samaria and Jerusalem.

1.1: Superscription. Among the Minor Prophets, this superscription is closest in form to those for Hosea (1.1), Amos (1.1), and Zephaniah (1.1). is a short form of the name Micaiah (1 Kings 22), which means, “Who is like Yahweh?” , a small town in southwest Judah (cf. 1.14), probably Tell el-Judeideh, 23 mi (37 km) southwest of Jerusalem. reigned from ca. 759 to 743 ; from 743/735 to 727/715; from 727/715 to 698/687 (the data are inconsistent for the reigns of Ahaz and Hezekiah). , Judean prophecy often had a visionary quality (e.g., Isa 1.1; Ezek 1.1.; Am 1.1; Ob 1; Nah 1.1; Hab 1.1).
Hear, you peoples, all of you; listen, O earth, and all that is in it; and let the Lord God be a witness against you, the Lord from his holy temple.
2 : , 1 Kings 22.28. , Isa 1:2. , Heb “Adonai YHWH.” , his heavenly abode; see Ps 11.4; Hab 2:20.
For lo, the Lord is coming out of his place, and will come down and tread upon the high places of the earth.
3 : , in general, hills and mountains (cf. Am 4.13), here perhaps with the specific connotation of illicit rural shrines.
Then the mountains will melt under him and the valleys will burst open, like wax near the fire, like waters poured down a steep place.
All this is for the transgression of Jacob and for the sins of the house of Israel. What is the transgression of Jacob? Is it not Samaria? And what is the high place of Judah? Is it not Jerusalem?

5 : , poetic synonym for Israel; cf. Gen 32.28. usually designates an open-air shrine but seems to refer here to the Solomonic Temple. The linked condemnation of Samaria and Jerusalem is also found in Am 6.1.
Therefore I will make Samaria a heap in the open country, a place for planting vineyards. I will pour down her stones into the valley, and uncover her foundations.

All her images shall be beaten to pieces, all her wages shall be burned with fire, and all her idols I will lay waste; for as the wages of a prostitute she gathered them, and as the wages of a prostitute they shall again be used.
1.2–7: The coming of the Lord. In grand style, the descends from heaven to punish Samaria. This oracle is probably to be dated before 722 , when Samaria fell to the Assyrians.
7 : This destruction of images is consistent with Deuteronomic law (Deut 7.25; 12.3). The phrase recalls the imagery of Hos 2.2–13; cf. Deut 23.18; Ezek 16.31.
For this I will lament and wail; I will go barefoot and naked; I will make lamentation like the jackals, and mourning like the ostriches.

8 : , conventional mourning behavior (2 Sam 15.30; Ezek 24.17,23), employed for effect by Micah's contemporary Isaiah (Isa 20.2). and , associated with desolation because they frequented ruins and produced eerie howls or cries; see also Job 30.29; Isa 34.13.
For her wound is incurable. It has come to Judah; it has reached to the gate of my people, to Jerusalem.
Tell it not in Gath, weep not at all; in Beth-leaphrah roll yourselves in the dust.
10 : , one of the five Philistine cities on the coast; 2 Sam 1.20b.
Pass on your way, inhabitants of Shaphir, in nakedness and shame; the inhabitants of Zaanan do not come forth; Beth-ezel is wailing and shall remove its support from you.
For the inhabitants of Maroth wait anxiously for good, yet disaster has come down from the Lord to the gate of Jerusalem.
Harness the steeds to the chariots, inhabitants of Lachish; it was the beginning of sin to daughter Zion, for in you were found the transgressions of Israel.
13 : Sennacherib's siege of , an important city 28 mi (45 km) southwest of Jerusalem, is depicted in Assyrian records.
Therefore you shall give parting gifts to Moresheth-gath; the houses of Achzib shall be a deception to the kings of Israel.
I will again bring a conqueror upon you, inhabitants of Mareshah; the glory of Israel shall come to Adullam.
15 : , later known as Marisa, is modern Tell Sandahanna, 3 mi (5 km) northeast of Lachish.
Make yourselves bald and cut off your hair for your pampered children; make yourselves as bald as the eagle, for they have gone from you into exile.

1.2–3.12: Speeches condemning Judean society and its leaders. This collection of speeches begins with an indictment of Samaria, capital of the Northern Kingdom, and ends with an indictment of Jerusalem, capital of the Southern Kingdom. This first major section of Micah is dominated by judgment speeches.
1.8–16: A lament for Samaria, and Jerusalem too.

1.10–16: These towns in southwestern Judah lay along the path of the Assyrian king Sennacherib's campaign in 701 (2 Kings 18–19; Isa 36–37), although the specific locations of several are unknown. There is a pun in virtually every verse of this section, lost in translation. To capture the wordplay in English one might translate v. 12: “the inhabitants of Bitterness wait … for something sweet.”

16 : , an extreme form of mourning behavior (e.g., Isa 15.2; Jer 7.29; 16.6; Am 8.10). (better, “children of your delight”) is not pejorative. It depicts the grief of parents bereft of exiled children. The , more exactly, the griffon vulture.
